speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 2, October 10, 1996, interview with Mrs. Pearl Barach. Tell me a little bit about life in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had friends from our town. It was-- it was hard. We didn't have too much food. We couldn't get too much. And I met with a-- with friends. And then, I got typhus. I was very sick for a few weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one night-- I don't know if it was true, or if it was dream-- I-- I dreamt that I went up-- up, and my brother opened the door. And he said, what are you doing here? Go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother told them I'm dying already. I was very sick. He said tell mother not to cry. It didn't hurt me much, it's just like a burn with a match. And he said, and I'm very happy here, but you go home, he said. And he said, the only thing I was embarrassed when Gentile people buried me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a very high fever?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very sick. I guess so. I didn't know what I was doing. And then, I woke up and I told my mother, you know, I just saw Eliezer. And she said, don't talk. Don't talk, you're too weak. But I said, no, I have to tell you what I saw and what he told. You shouldn't cry, and that it didn't hurt him. And he was-- you know, when he opened the door, I saw all the people with beards, religious people. And they were praying. And he was like the leader. And he-- he said he was very happy there. I told this to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he look in the dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. Very good. He looked happy. He told me he was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he look young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was young. Sure. He looked the same like he looked when he was alive. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many people get typhoid in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many people die at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, I don't remember. I don't know. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a gate around this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Near our house, it was the end of the ghetto. It went by-- went by us, by our-- by us, around the ghetto. And

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish police, inside. Outside, I don't know. We-- we weren't allowed to go outside together. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people-- were people allowed to go out of the houses at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not go out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any Germans coming into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, they came in. What they did, I don't remember. They-- they killed people. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see killings on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness any beatings in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were-- who was doing the beatings?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they just pick people to beat? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: They just told people to get out on the-- outside, and they beat them up. They called them for-- you know, they sent them away to camps, to concentration camps, slowly, before they liquidated the whole ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about liquidation of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in April, 1943, when the Warsaw ghetto uprising started, they started shipping out-- taking out the people and sent them to concentration camps. We had this wall, but we heard all the commotion and the shooting. They were shooting a lot. So we went and-- went down to this place, to the-- to the double wall. It was very tight, and very hot, very miserable, but we had to-- we tried to-- to-- to-- you know, to survive. And then, when it got quiet, we went out. And our house was right near the wires, so-- so we went-- we went-- we crossed, and there was a little river, and we crossed the river, and we started walking to our town. It was quiet-- we walked during the night. And during the day, we stayed in the woods, in the forest, whatever we can. What we ate, I don't remember. And-- and we took a few days, about a week, maybe. And we came-- because everybody was always promising that they will take-- they will hide us and they will take care of us. But then, it came-- we went-- my father went-- we went to the-- the different-- you know. Yeah. My brother, also-- I forgot to tell you that my brother came to the ghetto. He was an-- he had a Polish passport. But he didn't hear-- once in a while, he'd send a letter. And he gave it to the Jewish police, or to somebody. [INAUDIBLE] He'd send a letter, not through the post, not-- So-- but he didn't hear from us for a long time. He was worried. And he came into the ghetto. And he knew the policeman. They were friends-- the Jewish policeman-- so they let him in. And he came and stayed already with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this brother picked up the wires, and we all crawled under the wire. And we escaped. And we tried to walk during the night, because the Germans, you didn't see them during-- they were afraid too. And nobody saw it. Nobody saw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it took-- and then, we had not-- we didn't know what to do. We had no place where to go. We lost everything. So we said, we are going back to the ghetto. So we went back to the ghetto. It took us-- it took about-- we were-- were wandering around about three weeks, back and forth. And we came to the ghetto, we didn't have our house. The ghetto was made smaller, so we didn't have the wall. And now, months later, was another-- they liquidated again, the other people who were left. Some people survived the first. And then-- the first transport. And then, they told us all to go on the market, all the people. And everybody-- my brother had this passport, so he tried to put it in a loaf of bread. And other people put money, this, other things, maybe jewelry, I don't know. But the Germans took away all the breads, everything what we had. Nothing. And they took us to the-- to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- they collected you in the marketplace.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, took us to the trains. We walked to the stations. And they put us in the cattle cars. And-- and we were talking. My mother said, how old am I? I'm 42 years old. 42? It was 1943. She was young yet. And my two brothers were-- well, not two brothers, one, the oldest brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- what-- what was the name of the brother that with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham Chaim, the one who had the passport. And we were talking. And there was a little window. Only one little window in this car. And before the station, the train slows down. So we-- so my father said, you'll jump, and I'll-- and my brother will jump. I'll jump after him, and my mother will jump after me. So my father jumped. And my cousin was there, Shimon Friedman. So my father jumped, and my brother. And his cousin jumped. And my mother said, [? Pearl, ?] you jump. I said, no, I can't. I'm not going to jump. Because I was already-- I could not take it, the wandering around and no place where to go. I said, whatever will happen, will happen. And my mother didn't jump either. And my father's sister, with this little boy, was there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah Luba, with her little boy-- I don't even remember. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there any other relatives in the car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- my mother's sister, Pearl. Her name was Pearl. She was older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get out of that little window to jump?

speaker: interviewee
text: They opened the window, they jumped. It wasn't-- it was big enough to-- to get through. And the-- the train was going very slow, before every station, slowing down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear any Germans shooting them? Did the Germans see them escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we couldn't hear, because the-- they were on the roofs marching with the guns, but, I don't know, they-- they were all right. I-- my cousin told me that my father had a little scratch on the nose. And they got together. They-- they-- you know, whoever get-- you know, they got together, walking. And then, they walked to our town again. When they came, through the fields, they walked at night. And during the day, they stayed any place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they came-- they came to our town, on a-- to a farm. And they-- and they knew who it was. And it was a big barn. It was a barn there. And they went in there. And-- and they stayed during the day. The owner came. Victor [? Manobious ?] was his name. He came in. He said my father said to him, Victor, bring me a piece of bread. He said, OK, at night, I'll bring you a piece of bread. He came with a gun at night, and he started with my brother. And my father said, Victor, what are you doing? What are you doing? So he killed him, and then he killed my father. And my cousin-- it was dark-- and my cousin hid in the bushes. He could not find him. And when I came home, he told me all about it, how it happened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you, meanwhile, were still in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was in the cattle car. And I-- they took us to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how many days did the cattle car travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't-- it wasn't so far, maybe a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember when the cattle car door opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they opened? When the cattle car opened, we had to walk like from Lublin. It stopped in Lublin. Majdanek is about three kilometers, so we had to walk all this way. We came on-- came into the concentration camp. We had to take off-- first, they made a selection. My mother was holding this little-- my father's nephew, my cousin. And his mother was holding by the one hand. So they took them with the children, with all the people. They were still young, but they took them with them all. And me, they selected I get to work. And my mother's sister too came with me. And then they-- we took off the clothes, and we went to the showers, took off the clothes-- everything. And they looked us over. And we took showers. They gave us clean clothes-- different clothes-- one long-- big shoe, one small shoe, one high heel, one low heel. And-- and the-- and then, they took us to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the barracks like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barracks, like-- like, you know, like wood-- I-- how do I say it in English? Like bunk beds. Bunk beds? Just boards, one after the other. A lot. They were big and a lot of people in each. There was a [YIDDISH] there. She took care of the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a Jew, or a Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were Jews. There were-- some were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she act, the one in your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my barrack, not too good, but she had orders. She had to obey the orders too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What time did you have to wake up in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- early. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a roll call?

speaker: interviewee
text: A what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Roll call, a [GERMAN] appell?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- yeah. Sure. Oh, yes, every morning. We had to stand there until they counted if everybody's here. Yeah. And then, they took us to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we put the-- there were stones in one side, like the-- they told us to take it in a different direction. And then, the next day, they told us to take it someplace else, and then to the same place, back and forth, just to keep us busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding you while you worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever beaten?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anyone being shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shot? I saw they hung one girl. They took us all out, the whole camp, from all the barracks, and they were going to hang her. They said to us, don't say nothing, don't scream, don't yell, because-- and they said that she tried to escape. And if anybody will try to escape, they'll do the same thing. she-- they left her hanging like this. She was-- overnight. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see-- did-- did many of the girls die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, many. They took them out. They didn't die inside, they took them to-- they had like a little hospital, infirmary, whatever they called it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the number of your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember now. But I know we had the-- on the cloth. We didn't have our number on the arm, but on the cloth, we had a K L, concentration camp, in German, [? konzentrationslager. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of food did they give you to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, we had black coffee. In the afternoon, they gave us a soup with horse meat. And in the-- and at night, they gave us a slice of bread. So we did-- some people couldn't sleep, because they had a piece of bread. They were hungry. I used to try to save a little piece for the morning. And I-- I had-- I ate, and I had mine left for the morning. I should be, you know, not so hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of the women that were in your barrack with you in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. She was already married. She was [? Leah, ?] and Pearl, this Pearl [INAUDIBLE], Esther and her sister. Yeah, I knew a lot of-- [? and Malche. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the girls give each other courage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we always were thinking we'll survive. And you know, but it's all very sad. But we saw what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you talk about with your friends while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked about home, we had nobody, we were all alone, and what happened to our parents, and this, and that, you know? All kinds of-- we didn't have much time to talk, because we were working in the fields with the stones, carrying stones and brick, whatever they had there, not just because they needed it, but they wanted us to work for nothing. And-- and at night, you know, you-- we had to go-- be inside. We could not get out after the appell. At night, they also made an appell. They counted twice a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take at-- at nighttime? Were you outside of the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At night? No. We weren't allowed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness any act of kindness while you were in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the Germans? No. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was any one particularly cruel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were all cruel, but we tried not to get near them. We stayed away. Yeah, they-- they were beating people, the men, mostly. We saw the men from far-- they had different camps. They were in different-- men with men, and women with women. Yeah, I saw them. They beat a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were a lot of people dying around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- as I told you, they took them to the-- to the hospital, like.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have selections?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, they had selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us to-- to get out. And we stayed. And they-- you know, they-- they-- they looked over the people, they took away some. All the-- all the people in the-- and, well, whomever they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They probably took them to other camps, to Auschwitz, maybe. I don't know, they just took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Or maybe they-- they burned them, maybe to the ovens. I don't-- we didn't see that. I-- how could you see it? I saw the ovens. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the ovens like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ovens was like a big oven, and that's all. I don't know what they-- you know. [SWALLOWS HARD]

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you leave Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: How I left Majdanek-- I would say about four weeks to a month. And they registered people to go to the-- I mean, to-- to work to ammunition factories. But we could not believe them. But my aunt-- and I went to work, and my aunt was very mad at me. She said, where did you go today? They registered people to leave this camp, Majdanek. I said, you know what? In the afternoon, I won't go, and I'll register.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I registered, so she said, what did you do? You're going to death. I said, and what are you here? Not for that? I figured that I'll jump. And I knew where my father was supposed to go, to this [INAUDIBLE]. So I said-- she said, because of me, she has to go too. I said, if you want to go, you go. If you don't want to go, because of me, you don't have to go, but I'm going. But then, some-- and then, they selected-- they-- they looked us over. We had to strip, take off everything. She did too. I passed, she didn't. But then, I came the-- I didn't jump. They took us to Skarzysko from there. And how did you go from Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us in the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a cattle car again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Always with a cattle car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in the cattle car this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. From Majdanek to-- it was-- it was quite a few days without food, without nothing, because it's-- it was far, Lublin from--

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many girls were in the cattle car with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was packed. I don't know how many, but it was packed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to sit down?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't remember that, but it was packed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people talk to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we talked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we still didn't believe-- we didn't know where are we going. They told us where-- that we going to the ammunition factory, but we-- we didn't know anyway. But I-- I didn't want to jump.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened when the doors opened up again.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the doors opened up, there were police, their police, Germans, and-- and they took us to this camp. There were three camps, barrack A, barrack B, and barrack C. A was the best one. The B was-- I don't know, but-- but the C, they put in the-- the-- in the-- in the-- we made a shelf. And in the guns, they put in the powder. Oh, but they-- and the people got so yellow, they didn't have a bed-- a shower [? thing. ?] They used to take us to our camp to get showers, take showers. They were so yellow, like an egg yolk, from the powder, from the gun powder. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in camp A?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in camp A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many factories were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, was a big, big place. They took us with the-- with guards. They took us in the morning, and they brought-- and we came back at night, also with guards. But one week, I-- I worked nights-- they worked 24 hours a day-- and one week, days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the conditions of this camp better than Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was different, with smaller barracks. They were smaller barracks. And we could go outside and-- outside and talk to friends. And we're talking about what we talk about what happened to us. There's nothing else to talk. We had nobody, no-- they gave us food, a little better than Majdanek, a sweet soup every day. And the coffee in the morning, the same thing, a piece of bread. About the same. And they made selections. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the selections.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us to-- I-- to go outside and to line up. And they looked us over. Not naked, but like this. Whoever they didn't like, they-- I don't know where they took them. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you-- you never heard from them, the ones that were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no. First of all, I didn't know everybody. But from my town, we-- they were with me. A few people survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So about how many girls slept in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slept-- how many girls? I don't know, maybe-- oh, who knows? Maybe 50, 60? I-- I really don't remember how many. I remember, one woman was pregnant. And she had a baby on-- on the-- on the bed, on the bunk bed, if you're going to call it a bed. It wasn't-- and she had a baby, a little boy. And we were looking. And she didn't even-- she didn't say nothing. She had the baby, and they left it. And it got blue, and it died. They didn't do nothing to-- (QUIETLY) to him. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there men in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, but the men had separate barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of work did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they worked in the ammunition factories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see any beatings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I was beaten once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the meister, the German. He wore civilian clothes. And I-- and, you know, you made the-- the 40 millimeter. The shells we made, they were so big. And-- and when they don't come out right, they have a little scratch, they're not good. But by the time you pick it up-- they wear the hat, and they come out-- and by the time you pick it up, there are a few more [? schmeltz, ?] they used to say, no good. So he-- he-- he hit me in my face, in the eye, in the face. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have [GERMAN] appells here too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Appells? No, we-- we-- we-- we went outside, because we-- we didn't go by ourselves to the factory, with-- with guards. I guess it was an appell.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the winter time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oy, the winters were bad. We didn't have much warm clothes, and with no stockings. Whatever we came from Majdanek, that's all-- that's what we had. No stockings, no hats, no-- no-- no gloves, nothing. And we were freezing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a blanket?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blankets, they had-- we had. Oh, I-- I-- I covered myself with a sack from-- from flour they gave us-- you know, from-- from sugar.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to change tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how I'm doing. Kind of, I'm--
